AstroJohn 2026-02-10 8:25 a.m.
I have read the serial data from the Heltec V3 but cannot seem to make any sense of it - I am trying to look for frame code byte = /x08/ and I dont see any? Received: b'\xbb+^\xd9\x85\x19\xeb>#\x00\x886\xc0\t\n' Really odd not even hex? This is readline().
FDLamotte 2026-02-10 8:26 a.m.
this is a byte string
8:27 a.m.
if you want and hex string do b'bytes'.hex() (edited)
FDLamotte
if you want and hex string do b'bytes'.hex() (edited)
AstroJohn 2026-02-10 8:29 a.m.
Oh ok so: data = ser.readline().hex()
FDLamotte 2026-02-10 8:29 a.m.
@AstroJohn have you tried com2tcp ? I think this might be a good solution for the issues with the pyserial-asyncio on windows (edited)
AstroJohn
Oh ok so: data = ser.readline().hex()
FDLamotte 2026-02-10 8:29 a.m.
that's when you want to read it a string
8:29 a.m.
because for manipulating the bytes, you can just use it as an array
8:30 a.m.
there are methods to extracts ints from it
8:30 a.m.
or strs
8:32 a.m.
int.frombytes(buf[4:8], byteorder="little", signed=True) will get you the signed int at the 4th byte for example
8:34 a.m.
buf[16:].decode("utf-8", errors="ignore") will get you a string from 16th byte to the end
8:36 a.m.
There is also a very convenient tool, a buffered reader, in python it is io.BytesIO
8:36 a.m.
you initialize it with you byte array dbuf=io.BytesIO(data)
8:36 a.m.
and then you can do sequential reads on it using the read method (edited)
AstroJohn 2026-02-10 8:37 a.m.
Ok I am reading bytes now.. Not seen any 08 frames yet.. mostly 3e ? I have the protocol notes now though so I can look it up.
FDLamotte
and then you can do sequential reads on it using the read method (edited)
AstroJohn 2026-02-10 8:38 a.m.
Received: cf8abf7491f0047e04afdcb28fde18bc6db957b80c367ae49fbe6e2f6366 Received: 3e24008830be050bcf8abf7491f0047e04afdadcb28fde18bc6db957b80c367ae49fbe6e2f6366
FDLamotte 2026-02-10 8:43 a.m.
0x3e signals the begining of hte frame
8:44 a.m.
followed by the size
8:44 a.m.
so 3e2400 is just an header
8:44 a.m.
and then you have 08
8:45 a.m.
the newer doc forgets to mention that
8:45 a.m.
the old one is here (https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/wiki/Companion-Radio-Protocol)
Companion Radio Protocol
A new lightweight, hybrid routing mesh protocol for packet radios - meshcore-dev/MeshCore
1
8:46 a.m.
see : For USB - an outbound frame from starts with byte 62 (ASCII '>'), then 2 bytes with frame length (little-endian), followed by actual frame. An inbound frame starts with byte 60 (ASCII '<'), then 2 bytes with frame length, followed by actual frame.
FDLamotte
you initialize it with you byte array dbuf=io.BytesIO(data)
AstroJohn 2026-02-10 8:56 a.m.
Received: 3e8b008833c0110d940d110a ok so outbound frame 8b00 long? then the rest is text.
8:58 a.m.
No thats not right .. I am looking for 3e08???? where ???? is frame length
9:00 a.m.
So a channel message frame should be 3e08????yyyyyyyyyyyyy where Y is text?
FDLamotte 2026-02-10 9:07 a.m.
3e8b00 means you have a frame (3e) with a length of 0x008b so 139 bytes
9:10 a.m.
right I missed a 0
9:10 a.m.
so this is not 08, but 88 and it does not make sense
9:11 a.m.
even frame length does not make sense (if its a frame because I don't know what you've caught) you must be in the middle of a frame
9:12 a.m.
you'll need a first layer that gets valid inbound frames (starting with 3e)
9:14 a.m.
in meshcore_py this is done in handle_rx from serial_cx : https://github.com/meshcore-dev/meshcore_py/blob/eec63e4b3b73ce3d16715c95dc95a221fda2026b/src/meshcore/serial_cx.py#L75-L97
FDLamotte
3e8b00 means you have a frame (3e) with a length of 0x008b so 139 bytes
AstroJohn 2026-02-10 9:27 a.m.
Tricky isnt it. I will take a look at your code.. But still dont know why meshcore.py does not work.. More research!!
AstroJohn
Tricky isnt it. I will take a look at your code.. But still dont know why meshcore.py does not work.. More research!!
FDLamotte 2026-02-10 9:43 a.m.
on windows there are issues with ble connexion (mostly due to pairing) and the serial driver (I'm using pyserial-asyncio, for which the developers themselves issue a warning) that's why I think using the tcp driver might help this driver works either with a tcp-companion (which has to be built with wifi credentials) or using a serial/tcp bridge I've asked a pal who uses meshcore-cli on windows to validate the bridge option on his setup, I think I'll advise it over connecting via serial then on the README
9:44 a.m.
another option is to use WSL, as it will then use the linux version of pyserial-asyncio, which is known to work
9:47 a.m.
@everyone Just released meshcore_py v2.2.8 and mccli v1.4.1 as @pe1hvh solved a longstanding issue with low-level events (that were lost because sent before being waited) (edited)
1
FDLamotte
@everyone Just released meshcore_py v2.2.8 and mccli v1.4.1 as @pe1hvh solved a longstanding issue with low-level events (that were lost because sent before being waited) (edited)

AstroJohn
Does the new version get installed when I pip install meshcore?

you can do pip install --force-reinstall meshcore to be sure

you can do pip install --force-reinstall meshcore to be sure

AstroJohn 2026-02-10 3:12 p.m.
Received: 3e210080ef2e29599fc6db3a359feeb33fca2cf5af0a ok 3e I understand next two bytes are count.. Little endian so 0021 = dec 17bytes the 80 is little endian so 08 that is msg frame. so just got to decode ef2e29599fc6db3a359feeb33fca2cf5af0a as text??
AstroJohn 2026-02-10 3:37 p.m.
But this is a text string not a byte array?
FDLamotte
you can do pip install --force-reinstall meshcore to be sure
AstroJohn 2026-02-10 4:03 p.m.
I can find nothing in the protocol that says 08 is a msg frame? and what is a 88 frame? loads of them.
AstroJohn
But this is a text string not a byte array?
FDLamotte 2026-02-10 5:34 p.m.
told you hex() converts the byte array into a text string, ... you should manipulate the byte array directly, this is the raw data
5:34 p.m.
then I've given you the functions to get ints and strings from it
FDLamotte 2026-02-10 5:45 p.m.
There is another thing to know, is that to receive msgs from the node, you need to ask for them, issuing CMD_SYNC_MESSAGE (and it's also necessary to issue at least a CMD_APPSTART) at the begining of the connexion
5:45 p.m.
by default you'll only receive PUSH data
5:46 p.m.
there should be RX_LOG among them (if the firmware is configured to send it)
5:47 p.m.
and you can read messages from rx_log but they won't be removed from the queue in the device (and you'll have to decrypt them)
FDLamotte
There is another thing to know, is that to receive msgs from the node, you need to ask for them, issuing CMD_SYNC_MESSAGE (and it's also necessary to issue at least a CMD_APPSTART) at the begining of the connexion
AstroJohn 2026-02-10 6:19 p.m.

AstroJohn

5:01 a.m.

5:21 a.m.

AstroJohn 2026-02-11 5:41 a.m.

5:41 a.m.

FDLamotte 2026-02-11 6:51 a.m.
sometimes you need to refere to the source code https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/blob/e812632235274ffd2382adf5354168aec765d416/examples/companion_radio/MyMesh.cpp#L109 0x88 is LOG_RX, it's raw data received by the companion radio
6:54 a.m.
let me try to do a simple pure python example
6:58 a.m.
@AstroJohn I actually have something that might help you. I did a upython port of the lib that works in serial and tcp and does not use asyncio, it's a bit old but should still work for basic commands https://github.com/fdlamotte/meshcore_upy/blob/main/py_mcrun.py
meshcore_upy/py_mcrun.py at main · fdlamotte/meshcore_upy
Micropython bindings for meshcore. Contribute to fdlamotte/meshcore_upy development by creating an account on GitHub.
6:58 a.m.
and it can be used interactively
FDLamotte 2026-02-11 7:08 a.m.
Here is a sample log I've just done
7:08 a.m.
$ python Python 3.13.11 (main, Dec 8 2025, 11:43:54) [GCC 15.2.0] on linux Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information. >>> from py_mcrun import mc >>> mc.get_msg() {'code': 10, 'result': False} >>> mc.process_input() >>> mc.process_input() Received log data Msgs are waiting Received log data Msgs are waiting >>> mc.process_input() Received log data Received log data >>> mc.get_msg() Received log data {'code': 8, 'type': 'CHAN', 'channel_idx': 0, 'path_len': 0, 'txt_type': 0, 'sender_timestamp': 3799026793, 'text': 'Track-R: hello'} >>> mc.get_msg() {'code': 8, 'type': 'CHAN', 'channel_idx': 0, 'path_len': 0, 'txt_type': 0, 'sender_timestamp': 3815804009, 'text': "SDQ_FdL_Bot: Hola, mate! I'm SDQ_MCBot."} >>> mc.get_msg() {'code': 10, 'result': False} >>> mc.process_input() >>>
7:10 a.m.
just adjust pymcrun.py with your comport
7:14 a.m.
then sending a msg to a channel (and receiving a response) >>> mc.process_input() >>> mc.send_chan_msg(0, "hello") {'code': 0, 'result': True} >>> mc.process_input() Received log data Msgs are waiting Received log data Received log data >>> mc.process_input() >>> mc.get_msg() {'code': 8, 'type': 'CHAN', 'channel_idx': 0, 'path_len': 0, 'txt_type': 0, 'sender_timestamp': 1785891945, 'text': "SDQ_FdL_Bot: Aloha, bipedal mammal! I'm SDQ_MCBot."} >>> mc.get_msg() {'code': 10, 'result': False} >>>
7:16 a.m.
This code is based on pre v1.0 versions of meshcore-cli which was not event based ... it's a lot simpler
FDLamotte
sometimes you need to refere to the source code https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/blob/e812632235274ffd2382adf5354168aec765d416/examples/companion_radio/MyMesh.cpp#L109 0x88 is LOG_RX, it's raw data received by the companion radio

8:33 a.m.

FDLamotte 2026-02-11 8:35 a.m.
there is no package, only python scripts
8:35 a.m.
they call each other with import
8:36 a.m.
so if you run from the same dir it is ok
8:36 a.m.
or else I think there is something like a PYTHONPATH that you can set to tell python where to find them
FDLamotte
they call each other with import
AstroJohn 2026-02-11 8:36 a.m.

AstroJohn 2026-02-11 8:41 a.m.
Its ok I have downloaded to a folder.. I ran it and it just came back with >>>
FDLamotte 2026-02-11 8:42 a.m.
yes, now you can use python interactively to test
8:42 a.m.
from pymcrun import mc
8:42 a.m.
that will setup the connection
8:43 a.m.
mc.process_input() to proccess pending incoming frames
8:43 a.m.
mc.send_chan_msg(0, "test") to send test to chan 0 (public)
8:44 a.m.
mc.get_msg() to receive received_msg (edited)
8:44 a.m.
mc.wait_msg() to wait for a msg to arrive
8:45 a.m.
you can also write your script directly in pymcrun.py or copy it and write
FDLamotte
yes, now you can use python interactively to test

FDLamotte 2026-02-11 5:40 p.m.
@AstroJohn someone just raised an issue for meshcore_py and it's exactly the same kind of problem you have (receiving over serial blocking) https://github.com/meshcore-dev/meshcore_py/issues/54 hopefully I'll be able to reproduce and the fix
Serial framing parser loses sync on large packets, stops receiving ...
Description When receiving large radio packets (&gt;~130 bytes) via USB serial companion, the serial framing parser in serial_cx.py occasionally loses sync and permanently stops processing further ...
1
5:41 p.m.
this code is so old it is shared between meshcore_py and meshcore_upy
5:41 p.m.
don't know why I didn't notice it before. Maybe a difference between platforms
Just Stuff™ 2026-02-11 8:45 p.m.
I use this to discover repeaters in the Web app. mesh-utility.replit.app function buildNodeDiscoverReq(filter: number): Uint8Array { const tag = Math.floor(Math.random() * 0xFFFFFFFF); const data = new Uint8Array(1 + 1 + 1 + 4); data[0] = CMD_SEND_CONTROL_DATA; data[1] = CONTROL_NODE_DISCOVER_REQ | 0x01; data[2] = filter; data[3] = tag & 0xFF; data[4] = (tag >> 8) & 0xFF; data[5] = (tag >> 16) & 0xFF; data[6] = (tag >> 24) & 0xFF; remoteLog("log", `node_discover cmd: ${Array.from(data).map(b => b.toString(16).padStart(2, "0")).join(" ")}, filter=${filter}, tag=${tag}`); return data; } function parseControlDataFrame(frame: Uint8Array): NodeDiscoverEntry | null { if (frame.length < 5 || frame[0] !== PUSH_CODE_CONTROL_DATA) return null; const view = new DataView(frame.buffer, frame.byteOffset, frame.byteLength); const snrRaw = view.getInt8(1); const snr = snrRaw / 4; const rssi = view.getInt8(2); const pathLen = frame[3]; const payload = frame.slice(4); remoteLog("log", `ControlData raw: snr=${snr} rssi=${rssi} pathLen=${pathLen} payload(${payload.length}b)=${Array.from(payload).map(b => b.toString(16).padStart(2, "0")).join(" ")}`); if (payload.length < 1) { remoteLog("log", `ControlData: empty payload`); return null; } (edited)
FDLamotte
@AstroJohn someone just raised an issue for meshcore_py and it's exactly the same kind of problem you have (receiving over serial blocking) https://github.com/meshcore-dev/meshcore_py/issues/54 hopefully I'll be able to reproduce and the fix

Just Stuff™
I use this to discover repeaters in the Web app. mesh-utility.replit.app function buildNodeDiscoverReq(filter: number): Uint8Array { const tag = Math.floor(Math.random() * 0xFFFFFFFF); const data = new Uint8Array(1 + 1 + 1 + 4); data[0] = CMD_SEND_CONTROL_DATA; data[1] = CONTROL_NODE_DISCOVER_REQ | 0x01; data[2] = filter; data[3] = tag & 0xFF; data[4] = (tag >> 8) & 0xFF; data[5] = (tag >> 16) & 0xFF; data[6] = (tag >> 24) & 0xFF; remoteLog("log", `node_discover cmd: ${Array.from(data).map(b => b.toString(16).padStart(2, "0")).join(" ")}, filter=${filter}, tag=${tag}`); return data; } function parseControlDataFrame(frame: Uint8Array): NodeDiscoverEntry | null { if (frame.length < 5 || frame[0] !== PUSH_CODE_CONTROL_DATA) return null; const view = new DataView(frame.buffer, frame.byteOffset, frame.byteLength); const snrRaw = view.getInt8(1); const snr = snrRaw / 4; const rssi = view.getInt8(2); const pathLen = frame[3]; const payload = frame.slice(4); remoteLog("log", `ControlData raw: snr=${snr} rssi=${rssi} pathLen=${pathLen} payload(${payload.length}b)=${Array.from(payload).map(b => b.toString(16).padStart(2, "0")).join(" ")}`); if (payload.length < 1) { remoteLog("log", `ControlData: empty payload`); return null; } (edited)

FDLamotte 2026-02-13 6:43 a.m.
Thank you for the feedback, it's important. And also for the perseverance, after the testings we had done on meshcore_upy I would have rewritten the incriminated code to be more robust (the issue was just a confirmation)
FDLamotte 2026-02-13 6:24 p.m.
I just realized that with pip and pipx you can install directly from a git repo !
  • to install latest meshcore-cli from git : pipx install --force git+https://github.com/meshcore-dev/meshcore-cli.git
  • to install latest meshcore_py in your own environment : pip install --upgrade git+https://github.com/meshcore-dev/meshcore_py.git
  • to install latest meshcore_py in your meshcore-cli pipx install : pipx runpip meshcore-cli install --upgrade git+https://github.com/meshcore-dev/meshcore_py.git
Knowing that I'll probably release less often but more polished revisions to pypi
FDLamotte
I just realized that with pip and pipx you can install directly from a git repo !
  • to install latest meshcore-cli from git : pipx install --force git+https://github.com/meshcore-dev/meshcore-cli.git
  • to install latest meshcore_py in your own environment : pip install --upgrade git+https://github.com/meshcore-dev/meshcore_py.git
  • to install latest meshcore_py in your meshcore-cli pipx install : pipx runpip meshcore-cli install --upgrade git+https://github.com/meshcore-dev/meshcore_py.git
Knowing that I'll probably release less often but more polished revisions to pypi

FDLamotte 2026-02-14 6:36 a.m.
If you print the payload you should get the fields ... Here is what I get from mccli : $ mccli -j sm [{"type": "CHAN", "SNR": 10.5, "channel_idx": 0, "path_len": 0, "txt_type": 0, "sender_timestamp": 1771068821, "text": "SDQ_FdL_Bot: Ohayou gozaimasu, cellular automaton! I'm SDQ_MCBot."}]

yes, its a unix timestamp, there are python funcs to decode it

FDLamotte 2026-02-14 2:54 p.m.
@everyone mccli and meshcore_py now suppports repeat toggle on companion fw
  • get allowed_repeat_freq will show allowed freq for repeater mode
  • get radio shows repeater mode status
  • set radio accepts a new (facultative) parameter to set repeater status
2:56 p.m.
Track-R|*> get allowed_repeat_freq {"freqs": [{"min": 433000, "max": 433000}, {"min": 869000, "max": 869000}, {"min": 918000, "max": 918000}]} Track-R|*> set radio 918,62.5,8,8,on ok Track-R|*> get radio 918.0,62.5,8,8,on Track-R|*> set radio 910.525,62.5,8,8,off ok Track-R|*> get radio 910.525,62.5,8,8,off Track-R|*> set radio 910.525,62.5,8,8,on Error: Event(type=<EventType.ERROR: 'command_error'>, payload={'error_code': 6}, attributes={}) Track-R|*> (edited)
mcarper 2026-02-14 3:03 p.m.
Can repeater mode be on a timeout if on the primary frequency of the rest of the mesh? Max 7 days
FDLamotte 2026-02-14 3:15 p.m.
it would have to be supported on the hardware
3:16 p.m.
but there was a tempoorary set radio on repeaters, why not on companions ?
mcarper
Can repeater mode be on a timeout if on the primary frequency of the rest of the mesh? Max 7 days
Liam Cottle 2026-02-14 6:10 p.m.
Repeat mode is restricted to a set of frequencies that aren't currently used in the suggested radio presets list. So it's not possible to configure it to well known public mesh frequencies. Unless someone starts a new mesh on those frequencies. Which wouldn't be recommended, but would be an interesting experiment.
FDLamotte 2026-02-15 6:49 a.m.
v1.4.2 is out on pipx
FDLamotte
no it's because the firmware does not know you are using a protocol with snr ... (edited)
AstroJohn 2026-02-15 7:33 a.m.
I have set up #echo for the local new guys to test their setup. What would be nice is to be able to reply with path and snr. Not sure that meshcore_py can do that. I looked for a Trace_Data TRACE_DATA "trace_data" Route trace information Path with SNR data for each hop. Not sure how to get that?
7:34 a.m.
By all means try #echo ..
FDLamotte 2026-02-15 8:11 a.m.
@AstroJohn to get SNR you need to use send_device_query to let the firmware know you accept protocol > v3 For the path it is a little more tricky as it is not provided directly by the firmware in get_message, but can be obtained by listening rx_log, analyze packet and correlate them with message Meshcore_py doesn't do it, but lots of software based on it does, mc-ha, mcbot, mccli Here is how I display messages with their path in mccli (I don't do packet correlation, just print everything received) https://github.com/meshcore-dev/meshcore-cli/blob/5bc8379b52f356f1a890684d28553d7cc4041799/src/meshcore_cli/meshcore_cli.py#L232-L380
FDLamotte
@AstroJohn to get SNR you need to use send_device_query to let the firmware know you accept protocol > v3 For the path it is a little more tricky as it is not provided directly by the firmware in get_message, but can be obtained by listening rx_log, analyze packet and correlate them with message Meshcore_py doesn't do it, but lots of software based on it does, mc-ha, mcbot, mccli Here is how I display messages with their path in mccli (I don't do packet correlation, just print everything received) https://github.com/meshcore-dev/meshcore-cli/blob/5bc8379b52f356f1a890684d28553d7cc4041799/src/meshcore_cli/meshcore_cli.py#L232-L380

FDLamotte 2026-02-15 1:56 p.m.
@AstroJohn these are all the repeats you hear of the same message (from different repeaters), usually the one coming just before the new message event is the one your node received
[PNW] HerculesMulligan 2026-02-17 10:57 p.m.
I've been working on getting a virtual frame server working for pyMC_core. I think I might have stumbled across a discrepancy between meshcore_py and the firmware. MyMesh.cpp: ⁨⁨#define PUSH_CODE_CONTACT_DELETED 0x8F // used to notify client app of deleted contact when overwriting oldest⁩⁩ meshcore_py treats 0x8F as ADVERT_RESPONSE (advertisement/node-discovery response) in packets.py: ⁨⁨ADVERT_RESPONSE = 0x8F⁩⁩ From what I can see in meshcore_py, these aren't the same thing with two different names. Thoughts? (edited)

John 2026-03-04 4:33 a.m.
I have the software (meshcore_py) working ..give #echo a try.. just send a message . Next I have been requested to send path in echo . Thanks for your help..
John 2026-03-04 5:53 a.m.
Maybe a simple example of how to get a message path and rssi snr data?
John
Maybe a simple example of how to get a message path and rssi snr data?
FDLamotte 2026-03-04 6:37 a.m.
Hi John, path, snr and rssi are not given by the companion while reading message You need to get raw messages through log_rx (which is pushed by companion), parse them and correlate with the message in mccli I don't do correlation, just print log_rx, but there is a little parser for it (for channels and adverts) https://github.com/meshcore-dev/meshcore-cli/blob/db59c48b3f13f8fc91ac0905f2f8e33816b93385/src/meshcore_cli/meshcore_cli.py#L232-L385
FDLamotte
Hi John, path, snr and rssi are not given by the companion while reading message You need to get raw messages through log_rx (which is pushed by companion), parse them and correlate with the message in mccli I don't do correlation, just print log_rx, but there is a little parser for it (for channels and adverts) https://github.com/meshcore-dev/meshcore-cli/blob/db59c48b3f13f8fc91ac0905f2f8e33816b93385/src/meshcore_cli/meshcore_cli.py#L232-L385
AstroJohn 2026-03-04 9:53 a.m.
Seems very difficult to get path information for a message. Why was it made so complicated.. I guess meshcore would need to be rewritten to have a message id so you could request path for a message. I cannot understand what logs have to do with messages??
FDLamotte 2026-03-04 12:17 p.m.
well, that's not meshcore itself that makes it complicated, it's the companion radio protocole between the node and the application ... it was not meant to get the path, it was only later that the application got to need this information, and one way to get it is through the log_rx messages (which logs every meshcore packet that comes in the node)
12:18 p.m.
I could add the extra logic to get the path for message (when available) to the lib, it would then be transparent. Sadly I don't have time for it right now

11:12 a.m.

you'll have to request information for the channel you want to listen (so it knows how to decrypt) and also set mc.set_decrypt_channel_logs
AstroJohn 2026-03-05 11:20 a.m.
Where do I insert that in my code??
FDLamotte 2026-03-05 11:22 a.m.
if you have your echo channel as channel one, just do await mc.commands.get_channel(1)
11:22 a.m.
this will force the node to send channel info, and the reader will register it
11:23 a.m.
I'll make an example for receiving channel messages with path
FDLamotte 2026-03-05 11:33 a.m.
import asyncio from meshcore import MeshCore from meshcore import EventType from meshcore import BLEConnection async def main () : mc = await MeshCore.create_ble("f1x") mc.set_decrypt_channel_logs = True await mc.commands.get_channel(0) await mc.start_auto_message_fetching() while True: result = await mc.wait_for_event(EventType.CHANNEL_MSG_RECV) if result: print(result) asyncio.run(main())
11:33 a.m.
should show you the path when messages come through chan 0
FDLamotte
import asyncio from meshcore import MeshCore from meshcore import EventType from meshcore import BLEConnection async def main () : mc = await MeshCore.create_ble("f1x") mc.set_decrypt_channel_logs = True await mc.commands.get_channel(0) await mc.start_auto_message_fetching() while True: result = await mc.wait_for_event(EventType.CHANNEL_MSG_RECV) if result: print(result) asyncio.run(main())

AstroJohn 2026-03-13 10:16 a.m.
How does meshcore_py get 3 byte path info??
FDLamotte 2026-03-13 1:38 p.m.
You only get as many bytes as the sender put in its message ...
1:39 p.m.
you know the length from path_hash_mode
1:39 p.m.
path_hash_size = path_hash_mode + 1
FDLamotte
You only get as many bytes as the sender put in its message ...
AstroJohn 2026-03-14 6:27 a.m.
So I see people are using 'multibyte' now? Is this multi byte path?
FDLamotte
You only get as many bytes as the sender put in its message ...
AstroJohn 2026-03-14 6:31 a.m.
Is there a way to get multi byte paths in meshcore_py?
FDLamotte 2026-03-14 7:08 a.m.
there is commands.set_path_hash_mode(int) to configure the node to use multibyte
FDLamotte
there is commands.set_path_hash_mode(int) to configure the node to use multibyte
AstroJohn 2026-03-14 12:06 p.m.
Is that in meshcore_py?
FDLamotte 2026-03-14 3:09 p.m.
yes
FDLamotte
yes
AstroJohn 2026-03-17 7:12 a.m.
Ok what will that do if I set it to (2)? Will I get comma separated 2 byte paths?
AstroJohn
Ok what will that do if I set it to (2)? Will I get comma separated 2 byte paths?
FDLamotte 2026-03-17 7:45 a.m.
when receiving messages, path are coma separated (I directly put the value in the packet), if the path is a 2-bytes one you will have path_hash_mode=1 and path will be twice as long as it would have been
7:48 a.m.
in channel messages, it's the sender config that sets the path_hash_mode. If you set path_hash_mode=1 and the sender sends a message with a 1-byte path, you won't receive a 2-bytes path. If the receiver does not support multibyte and a multibyte message is received, the packet is discarded.
7:49 a.m.
and setting path_hash_mode=2 will configure the node to use 3-bytes path
7:52 a.m.
Regarding pathes for the contacts, it's another story. path_hash_mode is stored in the contact (in the device it is embedded in path_len (two upper bits)). So in the device you can have a mix of 1-byte, 2-byte and 3-byte path. If your device is configured with 2-bytes path and contact has a 1-bytes path stored, messages sent to that contact will be sent with a 1-byte path
7:54 a.m.
if path to a contact is not set (flood), then the flood message is sent using path_hash_mode config of the node, and the returned path will have a corresponding hash length
7:55 a.m.
so if your companion is configured with 3-bytes path and you send a flood message to a another device, this other device will send you the path to him using a 3-bytes path
7:56 a.m.
and all following messages you'll send to that companion will use this 3-bytes path (even if you change path_hash_mode afterwards)
7:57 a.m.
the reason why path_hash_mode is set to 0 for 1-bytes path is that when there were no multibytes, the two-upper bits of path_len (where is stored path_hash_size) were set to 0
2
accarda 2026-03-27 3:23 a.m.
Is it possible to send channel msgs with a regional scope using the USB companion with cmd chan <ch> <msg> after setting scope with cmd scope <scope> ? I have tried to set scope first but when I send a msg I see it with TRANSPORT_FLOOD_GROUP_TEXT but the msg is not repeated by a rpt which supports the same scope. Do I miss anything ? Thanks
FDLamotte 2026-03-27 7:18 p.m.
I will investigate
7:18 p.m.
normally if you're in the same session, the scope should be kept
7:18 p.m.
and I think I reset scope when entering interactive mode
7:19 p.m.
so it should also be kept between calls from cmdline
FDLamotte 2026-03-27 7:35 p.m.
well ... don't know why, but there seems to be an issue with transport codes ...
1
7:36 p.m.
it seems you need to prefix with # when setting scope
7:37 p.m.
event if the hashtags don't appear
7:37 p.m.
must be because I misunderstood how they were removed
7:37 p.m.
I'll change things so they are not mandatory
7:38 p.m.
but the hash must be calculated with hashtag and it's not done that way
1
FDLamotte 2026-03-27 8:13 p.m.
thanks for reporting @accarda it should now work by using meshcore_py v2.3.3
8:14 p.m.
if meshcore was installed using pipx, you can use pipx runpip meshcore-cli install -U meshcore to upgrade just meshcore_py
FDLamotte
if meshcore was installed using pipx, you can use pipx runpip meshcore-cli install -U meshcore to upgrade just meshcore_py
accarda 2026-03-27 11:46 p.m.
Thank you for the response, indeed I had to include the # when setting scope, with command: scope "#region" and now when message is sent, I can see it also being repeated. I haven't updated yet meshcore, but I'm testing with this # instead. Thanks again for the finding
accarda 2026-04-06 1:44 p.m.
Hello I'm running an app using meshcore-cli to fetch messages from an USB client (Heltec v3). Basically each x*seconds I'm polling the device with meshcore-cli -s USB -b 115200 sync_msgs to catch for any new message. The problem is that once I issue this command the OLED display turns on and then since the polling time is short it does not have the time to turn off before the next sycn_msgs. If I increase the polling time the display can shut for a while. I was wondering whether there is any command that I can send to disable the display while fetching msgs with sync_msgs ? I know from the Phone app there is an option in message settings that says: keep screen on - while viewing messages it keeps the screen on, but I'm not sure whether it's possible to handle this by cli and keep the display OFF while fetching messages. Thanks (edited)
accarda
Hello I'm running an app using meshcore-cli to fetch messages from an USB client (Heltec v3). Basically each x*seconds I'm polling the device with meshcore-cli -s USB -b 115200 sync_msgs to catch for any new message. The problem is that once I issue this command the OLED display turns on and then since the polling time is short it does not have the time to turn off before the next sycn_msgs. If I increase the polling time the display can shut for a while. I was wondering whether there is any command that I can send to disable the display while fetching msgs with sync_msgs ? I know from the Phone app there is an option in message settings that says: keep screen on - while viewing messages it keeps the screen on, but I'm not sure whether it's possible to handle this by cli and keep the display OFF while fetching messages. Thanks (edited)
FDLamotte 2026-04-07 10:46 a.m.
Hi, I've not digged into this functionality, at the moment this is not handled and I'll have to find where is the setting If it is a custom var it should be available using get custom that prints all the custom vars, if the setting is elsewhere it will have to be implemented specifically
FDLamotte
Hi, I've not digged into this functionality, at the moment this is not handled and I'll have to find where is the setting If it is a custom var it should be available using get custom that prints all the custom vars, if the setting is elsewhere it will have to be implemented specifically
accarda 2026-04-07 2:25 p.m.
Thanks for coming back. I have just tried to check get custom but I don't get any value, so it seems no custom values are set. I had set the companion via web app prior to connect it to raspberry pi and I made sure that option was not flagged. However I guess those options mostly work within the app. Anyway as you say it might be something that needs to be done at firmware level, but let me know if you find anything interesting. Thanks so much for now
FDLamotte
Hi, I've not digged into this functionality, at the moment this is not handled and I'll have to find where is the setting If it is a custom var it should be available using get custom that prints all the custom vars, if the setting is elsewhere it will have to be implemented specifically
accarda 2026-04-08 10:33 a.m.
Actually after looking at this little bit more, I guess the main issue is that the app keeps running commands that trigger the serial port to execute, so the device turns the OLED on with each interaction. I guess there is not much to do when taking this approach; probably a better approach would be to keep the serial communication opened and then send all commands in that session. So then the OLED should not trigger each time, as the serial is already open. I will look into it may be trying the interactive mode to test this approach. Update: using the interactive mode, I can issue the $sync_msgs to fetch messages and OLED stays off, as the session is already opened. I tried issuing the scope command to send channel msgs with scopes and it works fine now that I have updated to meshcore_py 2.3.5 and meshcore_cli 1.5.5. So this seems to me a viable solution to avoid that the OLED turns on each time. Now it stays off after entering the interactive mode. (edited)
Patrick 2026-04-16 4:10 p.m.
@FDLamotte Hey - this is not really about development but more about packaging and reaching more users. I'm a Ham and Debian Developer and started to create debian packages for meshore_py, and meshcore-cli in order to have a debian-native option to install these. For the work in progress look here: https://salsa.debian.org/debian-hamradio-team/python-meshcore and https://salsa.debian.org/debian-hamradio-team/meshcore-cli I'll maintain them within the debian-hamradio team, so that there are more than one person taking care of the packages. During packaging I found two dependencies which are not yet within debian cayennelpp and serial asyncio fast - the first one is also prepared as package, for the later one i currently patched it to use serial asyncio and provide the fast library in later stage. Would this be fine with you?
Patrick
@FDLamotte Hey - this is not really about development but more about packaging and reaching more users. I'm a Ham and Debian Developer and started to create debian packages for meshore_py, and meshcore-cli in order to have a debian-native option to install these. For the work in progress look here: https://salsa.debian.org/debian-hamradio-team/python-meshcore and https://salsa.debian.org/debian-hamradio-team/meshcore-cli I'll maintain them within the debian-hamradio team, so that there are more than one person taking care of the packages. During packaging I found two dependencies which are not yet within debian cayennelpp and serial asyncio fast - the first one is also prepared as package, for the later one i currently patched it to use serial asyncio and provide the fast library in later stage. Would this be fine with you?

7:18 a.m.

Patrick 2026-04-17 7:37 a.m.
What is your personal opinion on serial asyncio fast? To be faster ^^ I would patch it out for the first upload.
7:38 a.m.
And replace it with the default library
FDLamotte 2026-04-17 8:12 a.m.
the thing is that home assistant has dropped will drop support for legacy pyserial-async, supposedly because it wasn't updated, and they introduced their own "fast" version (edited)
8:14 a.m.
see https://github.com/meshcore-dev/meshcore_py/issues/40
pyserial-asyncio is deprecated, and will be blocked in Home Assista...
It should be replaced by pyserial-asyncio-fast: https://github.com/home-assistant-libs/pyserial-asyncio-fast See home-assistant/developers.home-assistant#2894 and home-assistant/core#116635
Patrick 2026-04-17 9:41 a.m.
That's no issue. It should not be a permanent solution. Currently there is no asyncio fast (yet) in debian.
Patrick 2026-04-18 8:16 a.m.
i've to revert my statement asyncio fast is indeed available as debian package - the package is slightly different named: python3-pyserial-asyncio-fast
8:16 a.m.
I'll use that
1
